title: When God Speaks in the Night
subtitle: On the Theology, Psychology, and Discernment of Dreams
description: On the Theology, Psychology, and Discernment of Dreams
body_html:
There is something sacred about falling asleep. We don’t normally think of it this way, because sleep feels automatic, but it is one of the most profound acts of trust we perform every day. We surrender control, awareness, and safety. We let go of consciousness and place ourselves in the hands of Someone who keeps watch.

Scripture says God “gives to His beloved sleep” (Ps 127:2). It is the picture of a Father guarding His child’s rest. And the night has always been one of the times when God speaks—not loudly, not dramatically, but tenderly, in the place where we are finally unguarded.

Dreams are one of the oldest languages of God. In the Bible, dreams appear everywhere: in patriarchs, prophets, kings, the Holy Family, the apostles, saints, and even the enemy camp. God uses dreams to guide, heal, reveal, warn, strengthen, and awaken the heart.

And in Catholic tradition, dreams have never been dismissed. St. Augustine wrote that “through dreams and visions God has made His will known.” St. Thomas Aquinas taught that God “sometimes reveals things to men in dreams” (Summa Theologiae II-II, Q.95, a.6). The Catechism teaches that the imagination is a faculty of prayer (CCC 2705). Saints like John Bosco had dreams that shaped entire movements.

Dreams aren’t superstition.

They are the soul speaking honestly.

And God meets us honestly.

Secular Minds Bumping Into Mystery

Even outside faith, the world keeps tripping over the mystery of dreams.

Some of the most important scientific and creative breakthroughs in history have reportedly come in the night. Dmitri Mendeleev said the periodic table finally crystallized for him in a dream after days of conscious struggle. August Kekulé described seeing a snake biting its own tail in a dream, which gave him the ring structure of benzene. Elias Howe told a story of solving the sewing machine needle by dreaming of spears with holes near their tips.

Paul McCartney woke up with the entire melody of “Yesterday” fully formed in his head—one of the most covered songs in human history. He was convinced he must have heard it somewhere before, because it arrived too complete to believe he’d composed it in his sleep.

Larry Page, co-founder of Google, famously said the entire idea of PageRank came to him in a dream at age 22. He woke up suddenly and scribbled down the architecture that became the backbone of the modern internet.

Einstein described dream-visions as a child of sliding down a beam of light—the exact image that eventually bloomed into his theory of relativity.

Inventors, artists, composers, and scientists often talk this way. They go to bed stuck, they wake up with a pattern, a structure, a melody, a solution.

If you only have a secular frame, you shrug and say, “Well, that’s just the subconscious.” Christians do not need to deny the brain to affirm more. Yes, God built our minds so that they keep working while we sleep. Yes, the unconscious continues to sort, associate, and integrate. But for those rooted in Christ, He is the filter through which all meaning passes. He is the Mind above our mind. He is the Logos who holds all patterns together. Whether someone acknowledges Him or not, every genuine insight comes through the One in whom “all things hold together” (Col 1:17).

Even the fathers of modern psychology could not escape this mystery. Sigmund Freud famously argued that dreams are wish fulfillments, never prophecy. For him, a truly prophetic dream would imply a personal God he could not accept, in large part because of his own experience of a weak and disappointing father. If “god” is just a projection of a fragile dad, then dreams cannot carry a future. And yet Freud himself dreamed of catastrophic war—imagery eerily similar to World War I—before it happened. He had no interpretive category for it. His own dream-life contradicted his atheism. His own unconscious refused the constraints of his theory.

Carl Jung went further. He saw archetypes, symbols, a collective unconscious. He had his own visions of Europe flooded with blood years before the war. He understood that dreams often speak with a gravity that exceeds personal desire. But even Jung, for all his brilliance, stopped short of the Incarnation. He saw pattern, not Person. Structure, not Savior.

The Christian claim is different. We are not surprised that brilliant unbelievers dream their way into discoveries. We are not offended that secular psychology can describe parts of the mechanism. We simply insist that grace has always been more than mechanism. The same God who gives rain to the just and unjust also gives ideas in the night. The same Christ who upholds the cosmos also meets His children in their sleep.

For the believer, this means something huge.

Even when your brain is “just processing,” Jesus is the One holding you. He is the true Dreamcatcher over your bed, not the sentimental hoop with feathers, but the Crucified and Risen Lord whose Cross stands above your nights and whose banner of love is over you as you sleep (Song 2:4). You fall into bed, you lose control, your body resets, your memories reshuffle, but you do not fall out of His hands.

Every dream, whether you can interpret it or not, unfolds under that mercy.

My Journey Into Dreams

I wasn’t born a dreamer. I didn’t wake up as a child with symbolic images or prophetic scenes. For most of my life, I slept deeply and woke up blank. Dreams weren’t part of my spirituality or my psychology. I didn’t have categories for how God might speak at night.

But as an adult, something shifted.

Nicole dreamed often. Clients dreamed vividly. People I ministered to would bring me dreams filled with emotional truth. I would listen and help them discern, but I felt something inside myself I hadn’t yet named.

I was hungry.

I was jealous in the holy sense.

I wanted to hear God too.

This was before Encounter, before the deliverance ministry side of my life, before everything that came later. I began studying dreams because I sensed God speaking to others in a language I did not understand yet.

I learned from John Paul Jackson, from The Divinity Code, from Sean Bolz’s dream interpretation sessions. I didn’t want the skill of interpreting dreams. I wanted the intimacy that produced them. I wanted my own inner world to be a place where God met me.

And then, in late February 2020—before I knew anything about COVID—I had a dream that changed everything for me.

This dream did not predict the future.

It prepared my heart.

It shaped how I walked through the pandemic.

It stayed with me the entire time.

Every Dream Becomes Meaningful When Brought to God

One of the most healing truths about dreams is this:

Every dream becomes meaningful the moment you bring it to God.

Not because every dream is divine, but because you are.

When my kids wake up and tell me their dreams—whether silly, random, symbolic, or frightening—I don’t reject them. I delight in them. We laugh, or we talk about their feelings, or I help them understand something deeper they didn’t realize they were expressing.

Every dream becomes meaningful because I love them.

The conversation matters more than the content.

This is how God receives you.

Your dreams are not a test.

They are the place where your heart speaks honestly.

And God meets you honestly.

Even nightmares can become places of intimacy.

Even “pizza dreams” become places of joy.

Even demonic imagery becomes a place of authority and courage.

Nothing is wasted when placed in God’s hands.

Not even the night.

Share

The Theology of Dreams

Job tells us that God speaks “in a dream, in a vision of the night” to open the ear of the heart (Job 33:14–18). The night exposes the parts of us that hide during the day. The subconscious rises. Memory reorganizes. Wounds surface. Desire speaks. The imagination awakens, and the spirit becomes attentive.

Dreams are not less spiritual because they are psychological.

They are more spiritual because they are human.

Scripture is full of dreams:

Abraham receiving covenant revelation. Jacob seeing a ladder connecting heaven and earth. Joseph dreaming of destiny. Pharaoh dreaming of famine. Solomon receiving wisdom. Nebuchadnezzar receiving warnings. Daniel interpreting empires. Joseph (husband of Mary) receiving four directive dreams. The Magi being warned. Pilate’s wife being troubled. Peter’s rooftop vision that opened salvation to the Gentiles. Paul being redirected by dreams. John receiving Revelation.

Even Gideon received courage through overhearing the enemy’s dream (Judges 7:13–15). Sometimes God lets you hear the enemy’s “nightmares” about your calling so that you rise into your identity.

Dreams are not marginal.

Dreams are relational.

Dreams reveal how God fathers the heart.

Catholic Tradition Weaves Peacefully With This

The saints never feared dreams.

They discerned them.

Augustine wrote, “The inner man sees according to the images of the heart.”

Aquinas taught that God uses the imagination while it is at rest.

The Catechism affirms imagination’s place in prayer.

John Bosco used symbolic dreams to guide orphan boys into holiness.

Catholic spirituality is sacramental, symbolic, incarnational.

Dreams fit naturally inside that world.

They are not a foreign practice—they are the heart speaking symbolically to the God who loves symbols.

Dreams and Maturity

Joel’s line about “old men dreaming dreams” is not primarily about biological age. It is about maturity. A mature spiritual life is unhurried. It is able to rest. It understands that God speaks more clearly when striving stops. The older you become in Christ, the more restful your listening becomes.

Dreams become easier to hear not when you become more spiritual, but when you become more childlike in your trust.

Rest becomes productive.

Peace becomes fruitful.

Time with God becomes the work.

And sleep becomes the sanctuary where God plants seeds for the day to come.

The Dream I Had Before the World Changed

In late February 2020 — before I had heard a single thing personally about COVID, I had a dream that felt like a parable being spoken in symbols. It wasn’t literal. It wasn’t linear. It came as atmosphere, imagery, and emotion — like revelation wrapped in story.

In the dream, I stood on a hillside overlooking a sprawling city. The skyline was dimmed, hazy, almost washed out, like something in the spiritual air had thinned. Then I saw something that made my stomach drop:

People were fleeing the city.

Not in fear, not in chaos. Just moving—quietly, steadily—in long streams, families and individuals walking away with whatever they could carry.

It didn’t feel like fleeing a disaster. It felt like people leaving a place that had become spiritually inhospitable.

Then, without words, I suddenly understood what was happening: The virus was spreading through what people were eating.

Not literal food. Symbolic food. The things they consumed. The spiritual diet. The emotional diet. The cultural diet.

The dream was saying: People were being infected by the very things they fed their souls on. Fear. Distraction. Division. Anger. Noise. What they consumed inwardly became the contagion outwardly.

The scene shifted.

Suddenly I stood in a vast desert. Empty. Exposed. Silent.

In the middle of that desert was a massive spaceship that had crash-landed—metal twisted, hull broken, half-buried in sand.

But the moment I looked at it, I knew exactly what it represented:

The ship was the Church.

Not the Bride in glory. The institutional Church—its systems, its structures, its machinery, its engines, its momentum—all crash-landed in a barren place.

It wasn’t destroyed. But it was grounded. Unable to fly. Unable to operate the way it once had.

It was like the Spirit was showing me that a season was coming where the Church would be stripped of its machinery, its busyness, its noise, its programs, its momentum—and forced into the desert to rediscover its dependence.

Around the wreckage stood people—confused, grounded, unsure of what to do next. It wasn’t panic. It was revelation: We can’t keep doing Church the way we were.

As I walked past the crash-landed ship, I approached a small group of Black men gathered together. They looked tired but steady—resilient, grounded, carrying a calm clarity in the middle of the desert.

One of them looked at me and asked who I was.

I said, “I’m a psychologist. I help people like you.”

He nodded, paused, reached into his pocket, and handed me five dollars.

Not asking for anything. Not trying to get help. Not begging.

Giving.

It wasn’t about money. It was symbolic—almost like a sign of honor, or recognition, or commissioning.

It felt like: “In the desert, your work matters. And we see it. Take this—because you will need to give, and you will need to receive.”

It carried dignity. It carried blessing. It carried the sense that the people we often consider “on the margins” would actually be the ones to recognize and value the healing the Church would need in the wilderness.

The entire dream held an atmosphere of stripping:

• People fleeing the city → leaving unhealthy spiritual environments

• Virus through eating → the exposure of what we inwardly consume

• The spaceship → the Church crash-landing where God could rebuild it

• The desert → purification, humility, rediscovering dependence

• The $5 gift → honor, value, commissioning, the human face of crisis

When I woke up, I didn’t know what the dream meant. But I felt its weight.

Days later, COVID hit the news. And the dream suddenly made sense—not as prediction, but as revelation:

• People did flee the rhythms and structures of normal life.

• What we consumed inwardly did become the real contagion—fear, rage, confusion, division.

• The Church did crash-land, in a sense—forced out of its buildings, programs, and habits into a desert of simplicity—even projecting its message virtually.

• Communities wounded by society did become prophets of resilience, dignity, and grounded truth.

• And healing work did become essential, not optional.

The dream didn’t tell me what would happen. It told me how to walk through it. It shaped my posture. It prepared my spirit for a world—and a Church—about to be humbled, exposed, and invited back to the desert where God rebuilds His people.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

When the pandemic hit, I recognized the atmosphere before I recognized the crisis.

Subscribe now

What That Dream Revealed About How God Speaks

Dreams don’t always arrive with clarity. Sometimes they arrive with atmosphere. Sometimes with emotional truth. Sometimes with symbolic imagery that only makes sense months or years later. But they often reveal what the heart senses long before the mind understands it.

Psychology affirms this. During sleep, the emotional brain activates, the logical brain quiets, memories reorganize, and the subconscious rises. Dreams speak the language of emotion, not logic. And that is precisely why God uses them. He speaks to the part of you that’s too defended during the day.

Augustine wrote that “the inner man sees according to the images of the heart.” Aquinas taught that God can reveal truths when the imagination is open and unguarded. The Catechism says the imagination is a legitimate place of prayer (CCC 2705). Dreams honor the emotional truth of your heart. And God honors the whole person.

But the most important thing to understand is this: A dream becomes meaningful when you bring it to God. Not because it was divine, but because He delights in the child who shares their heart.

This is how God fathers us. He listens the way a parent listens.

When my children wake up and tell me their dreams—whether funny, bizarre, frightening, or random—I never dismiss them. I delight in them. I help them name what they felt. I sometimes sense a deeper truth beneath the imagery. But above all, I receive their dreams because I receive them.

This is how God receives you. Your dreams are not tests. They are openings. They are invitations. They are places where God waits for you to speak.

This is why even “negative” dreams matter. Nightmares often reveal the wounds that need healing. Oppressive dreams often expose the enemy’s fear of your calling. Confusing dreams often highlight something you’re not ready to articulate.

Job says God can “terrify” us in dreams to turn us from danger or deception (Job 33:14–18). In Judges 7, God strengthens Gideon by letting him overhear the enemy’s dream—literally the enemy’s fear of Gideon’s destiny. In Genesis, Joseph’s dreams guide entire nations. In Acts, Peter’s rooftop dream expands salvation history.

And Joel says that “old men will dream dreams,” meaning mature believers will walk in a rest-filled spirituality where sleep becomes fruitful. At a certain point in spiritual maturity, striving ceases and relational trust deepens. You begin to discover that time with God—including resting with Him—accomplishes more than working without Him.

This is maturity: to let God speak in the quiet, to trust Him with your vulnerability, to let Him guide through parable rather than blueprint, to let rest become part of your productivity, to let sleep become an act of surrender. Dreams are where that surrender becomes intimate.

Dreams Reveal the Conversations We Avoid During the Day

During waking hours, your ego is active. You manage impressions. You stay busy. You distract yourself. You protect yourself. You avoid certain truths. You push away certain emotions.

But at night, all of that softens. The subconscious rises. Memory surfaces. Emotion unfolds. The imagination lights up. And the spirit listens.

Dreams are where the unfinished conversations in your heart finally come to the surface. God doesn’t force revelation. He waits for vulnerability. And vulnerability often arrives at night.

Dreams are not always easy. But they are always relational. They show you the truth of where your heart is. And God speaks into that truth with tenderness.

Dreams as Sacrament of Rest

Catholic spirituality is sacramental. It believes that God uses material, emotional, symbolic things to reveal invisible grace. Dreams follow the same pattern.

Rest is not wasted time. Rest is the place where God restores, reveals, and forms. This is why Joel links dreams with maturity. This is why saints emphasize contemplation. This is why Jesus says the kingdom belongs to children. This is why Heidi Baker teaches that an hour with God can accomplish more than a day of striving.

Dreams remind you that you are held. They remind you that God is working while you sleep. They remind you that the spiritual life is not a performance—it is a relationship.

(What follows: a complete practical guide to discerning your dreams—7 core principles for listening with God, a dictionary of common dream themes and their invitations, gentle practices for bringing dreams into prayer, and a blessing for your nights. Plus future deep dives on dream journaling, pattern-mapping, trauma dreams vs God dreams, and real case studies from ministry.)
